regions.label: text
regions.text: [OCR timeout]

regions.label: text
regions.text: Knoxville, Tennessee, July 21. A river man on the Tennessee river, a mile below the oily, discovered the body of a woman floating down the river on last Monday afternoon. His rower, where he identified himself, to the river, identification was impossible at the time due to the state of decomposition, and it was prepared for burial by the undertaking establishment of Hamilton & Lillison. Interment followed in the county cemetery. Wednesday Earnest Walker appeared before the coroner, and testified that the body found was that of his wife, Mrs. Daisy Walker, having identified it by certain gold teeth, and by other teeth that were decayed. Mrs. Mary Smith, the coroner, identified her, Ida Smith, also identified the body as being that of the Walker woman.

regions.label: text
regions.text: The verdict of the coroner's jury was that the woman's name was Daisy Walker and that she came to her death by doorstep. The Walker woman suddenly disappeared from her home on Saxon street on Fob, 11. The police were notified and a picture of the woman was furnished them, but no trace was found of her. The body was taken in charge by the husband and burial in another cemetery.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Cincinnati, Ohio, July 21.—Moses Friedman, a salesman in one of the downtown stores, was dismissed in the juvenile Court, contributing to the delinquency of a young Race girl. The story of the girl was a pitiful one. The classroom was packed when the trial was ended. Friedman was a house-to-house salesman and in his rounds visited a number of homes. He knew why he was doing this time that the criminally assaulted the young girl. The case was so horrifying that it was sent to the Criminal court. The matter was turned over to Prosecutor Campbell, but nothing will be done unless the middle class of people get behind the case and pitch it to the last degree. It is said here that the "Big Negro" is too much interested in schools to take in such a person, and are mostly interested in getting their daughters jobs rather than come to the rescue of an assaulted girl of their Race.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: WANT RACE MAN

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: ON SCHOOL BOARD

regions.label: text
regions.text: Louisville, Ky., July 21. —Sentiment is being aroused here among members of the race to elect next year a member of the board of education. The whites have representatives among the aristocrats and the middle class, but to no one interests. Articles from the ten of W. H. Goodall are being written weekly to arouse the people toward their duty along educational lines. The best they can do is to stand on outside and say, My name is Jimmy; I'll take what you give me. You'll drop all past differences and that wives, sweethearts, cousins, aunts, take a long pull, or strong pull and a pull together and effect one.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: BASKET MEETING HELD

regions.label: text
regions.text: Weston, W. M. July 21. Quite a large number of our guests attended a basket meeting held near Buckhamon Sunday. All report an enjoyable time, with plenty of eat. Miss Emma Barnett, an employed of the hospital, was in Clarkstarg or Business last week. Mr. Wm. Carpent, popular waiter of the Chancery district, was here last week. Master Geoff B. spent Sunday at Buckhamon among friends. Mr. Simpson Brown writes from the Johns Hopkins hospital, where he has been confined for the past three weeks, that he is on the road to recovery and that he will soon be with us again, for which the writer and his wife have written. Miss Jenne Littleton of Clarkstarg spent day here among friends. Mrs. Laura Tone, an employee of the hospital, returned from Pomeroy, Ohio, Saturday, where she had been for the past four weeks with her mother, who has been in poor health. She reports her condition much improved and that she will soon be well. Mr. Robert Riddles, our popular Carden Club member, has few days visiting. Mr. McNeil who was formerly in the pressing business here, but now located at Morguntown, spent Sunday here with his family on Stone Coal. Mrs. J. B. Tanner and son, Ullyses, Los Angeles, Calif., are the honored guests of Mrs. Martha Tanner and Mrs. Comma Bailey, a center street. Mrs. Carma arrived here on Saturday to remain for two days at St. Paul's A. M. E. Church will give a sacredcert in the near future. Miss Lily Robinson and Miss Jamie Muckey attended a dance at Clarkstarg on Friday. They report a plea at time. Miss May Daniel, who had been employed here for the past week, returned to her home at St. Mary, M. G. C. Jenkins, our pastress loyal, represented the Milt Missionary Society, who wcheduled last week. Mr. and Mrs. Chrrence Lacey of Clarkstarg spent Sunday here with home folks.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: AUTO ACCIDENT

regions.label: text
regions.text: Brooklyn, N. Y., July 21.—On last Wednesday afternoon, Mr. Louis Thompson of 255 Montauk avenue, East New York, while speeding along Fulton street, Jamaica, L. L., in his new car, with a party of friends ran into Harry Felder, white, of 125 Rockaway road, Jamaica, who was riding a wheel. Mr. Thompson, who is a very careful as well as an elegant chauffeur, blew his horn several

regions.label: text
regions.text: trip, but Folder seemed to pay no attention to it, the car smiling into the room.

regions.label: text
regions.text: on wheale, tender got up, full of night, and he wandered around, or his friends whispered in his ear that he had better try to get something for it. A large crowd gathered when the ambulance from the Jamaica hospital arrived, which had been sent for some time before. Dr. Arcobasse, the ambulance officer, explained there was nothing serious outside of a few bruises, and contusion of the skin, but he insisted on going to the hospital. A policeman in plain clothes, who was an eye-witness to the accident uncompromised Mr. Thompson, 57, who had been situated in the town hall, and explained the occurrence to the captain, who released him from all blame.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: HOTEL MAN IN TROUBLE

regions.label: text
regions.text: Longbranch, N. J.; July 21—Warrants were issued for Mr. and Mrs. Edward Banks, the only licensed hotel in this county, by a white automobile dealer, charging the hotel man with assault and battery. It seems as there has been trouble brewing for some time, and a fight last Saturday was the result. The white auto dealers tried to take the auto away from Banks and his wife, and the hotel owners claimed he acted in self-defense.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: AWARDED PRIZES

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Philadelphia, Pa., July 21—The prizes
put up by the Main Line Citizens' association (white) for general improvements in home conditions, modern baby care and recreation, respectively to Mrs. Silas Johnson of 714 rocks street, to her son, William; Mrs. Callett, to her daughter, Ethel, and Mrs. William Smith to her son, John.

MUST HAVE $25,000

There are so many come

regions.label: text
regions.text: Atlantic City, N. J., July 21—Articles for publication in this column may be addressed or left at the Arctic avenue branch, Y. M. C. A. * The Defender is on sale at all the news stands. at 12 o'clock Saturdays. On sale at Satchel's Michigan avenue; Thompson's, Michigan avenue; Mathews', 1811 Atlantic avenue; Pinkert's, 1803 Arctic avenue; Brock's, 1704 Arctic avenue; Mariah's lunchroom, Kentucky avenue; Gordon Brown's barber shop; Morris's pharmacy, New York avenue; Buchanan's, 1413 Arctic avenue; Fred Delaney's, Pennsylvania and Baltic avenues; Larkin's Delaware and Baltic avenues. Subscription rates are $1.50 a year, $1.00 six months. Notify carrier or address 112 North Ohio avenue, if you wish to take out a subscription. Visitors to the home cannot secure the Defender at home would find it to their advantage to get in communication with the agent. The Entre-Nous dancing classes continue to tap into a large and rehired patronage. The Navy Shops in Chestnut furnishies the music for the class attendance always has a good time. the Chicago Defender, after a ten years' struggle, has reached the top in Race journalism. We are now publishing the only 12-page 7-column journal in circuit journalism. and seven columns in the Shops coast, and from the domain to Panama. No other paper covers such a wide range of territory. And, of course, if you want to know what's going on at the Shore, read the Defender. * The appointment of Thomas Jackson as a health inspector met with the hearty approval of all citizens. * Mrs. Odesa Bonner of New York city is the Shore commissioner of the James River, proclaimed the popular Sandy Springs hotel, has returned to the Shore after a two weeks' visit to relatives in Baltimore and Washington. * Mrs. George Press and daughter, Miss Reba Press, have returned to Philadelphia at a three weeks' sojourn at the Shore. * Mr. Walter Trower and friend, from Philadelphia, have returned to Philadelphia. * Miss Lordella Chew has returned to Philadelphia after a two weeks' sojourn in Atlantic City. * The second of a series of sacred concerts will be given at St. Augustine's church, 1708 Arctic avenue, Sunday, July 30, from 4 to 5 p.m. Watch next week's Defender for the program. Miss Chew defends the Arctic avenue, which is spending the summer at the seashore. Mr. and Mrs. Wilkens are in the City-the-Sen for a week. They are registered at the Satchel cottage, 27 North Michigan avenue.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: K. of P. Installation

regions.label: text
regions.text: Last Friday evening Dionysius lodge, No. 37, K. of P., held their semi-annual installation of officers. The following officers were installed for the following term: Chancellor commander, John L. June; vice chancellor, Samuel Murray; keeper of records and seals, J. Chan; Jackson; master of finance, and Bell Jackson; master of finance, Cozart; prelate, Joseph McGaen; master-at-arms, Loo Smith; inner guard, Preston Barnes; outer guard, George Harris; trustees, Robert Poole and Columbus Hutchins. Dionysius lodge is considered to be one of the thriftiest lodges of the state, being installed March 18, 1812, being installed March 18, 1815, field Cozart, who will be remembered as the fighting progressive delegate to the national convention recently held in Chicago. The installation ceremonies conducted by District Deputy Clifton

regions.label: text
regions.text:
was placed on the roster c
after first leucommens a few days.
after confirmation of their appointments by
Adjutant General Dickson in Springfield,
Ill.: Serricant Major Eugene P. Priferon.
Tenth cavalry, who was with his regiment, has been in the United States army twenty-two years without the loss of a single day, and with the exception of a gunshot wound in the right leg has never been on the sick list. He is an expert rifle and pistol shot, has won medals in competition with riflemen and during the year 1908 was selected on the national lists from Manila, F. L. to the United States from New York, the national rifle and revolver competitions, afterward returning to the Philippines, having traveled during the year 22,500 miles by rail and water. In 1909 his regiment salled around the world; the Tenth being the only regiment of the race with this fact to its credit, and done so in the same manner ever been accorded this opportunity. He has been a member of the regimental non-missioned staff of the Tenth for twelve years and sergeant major for six years. During his twenty-two years service he has traveled on he sech 8,550 miles. His duties as sergeant are the same as clerk in civil life. Serricant Renggent, a machine gun company, at host Sam Houston, was made lieutenant. He has a wonderful record with his command. Two sergeants are picked from the Twenty-fourth infantry, one Serricant William B. Crawford, L company, his regiment at Mexico; first Serricant, Twenty-fourth Infantry, Green, K company, Twenty-fourth Infantry, Green, K company. These men have not allowed to go through the army schools of the United States; can't get in at West Point or Annapolis, but there is

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Dorsey and Special Deputy Alexander Jeffries.

regions.label: text
regions.text: Mrs. Mamie Bowers of Pittsburgh, Pa., addressed the B. Y. P. of the Second Baptist church last Sunday. Her topic, Purity, Temperance and Strength, held after many in attendance. After the address, P. Larry Robert, W. Watts of Petersburg, R. W. and Charles Douglas joined in discussing the topic. Miss Eleanor Terry rendered a beautiful solo. Six new members were added to the Union. At the next Sunday, July 23, the subject will be *Enthusiasm.* Silvia Williams, 2-year-old infant, residing at 222½ North Illinois avenue, had a narrow escape from death last Monday when she was run down, by a hitmen. After a hurried trip to the city she and she had miraculously escaped with few minor bruises. * The St. James.* Sunday school gave their annual picnic at Mays Landing last Tuesday. A large number journeyed to the mainland and a pleasant time was recorded by all. * For rent, with use of kitchen and dining room,* minutes from Penn, and electric stations. For particulars write Mrs. Louis Collins. 121½ Tennessee avenue.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: MAKES APPEAL TO JUDGE

regions.label: text
regions.text:
MAKES APPEAL TO JUDGE
Galveston, Texas, July 21. Last year, this section of the country was visited by a terrible flood and its greatest dam was among the members of the Race. Given in the outrage and martial law was decided this time members of the Race were imprisoned and all kinds of ill treatment attended them. E. L. Branch, one of the leading members of the Race in this section, is doing his very best to get justice on the bar and as chairman of the legal committee. The following letter is addressed to Judge Walter T. Burns:

regions.label: text
regions.text: [OCR timeout]

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: BREAKS AND RUNS; IS

regions.label: text
regions.text: Saturday morning of last week William Fox, 2518 Federal street, had an altercation with Bishop Williams, an officer of the law who travels out of the Cottage Grove avenue station. Fox, the officer claims, broke away and ran. The officer started after his man, but he refused to stop when called upon and the officer fired, shooting Fox through the window. He was taken by Bridwell hospital, where his wife stated to a Defender reporter that he is improving nicely. Mrs. Fox, up to Monday night, had not learned anything definite about the trouble.

regions.text:
IANT FRIERSON

Regular army man who is given commission in the Eighth Regiment

regions.label: text
regions.text: [OCR timeout]

regions.label: doc_title
regions.text: ROBERT GRAHAM, 19, IS SENTENCED TO PONTIAC

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Robert Graham, aged 19, who robbed Mrs. Alice Turner, 4622 State Street, on July 8, was tried in the Boyle court, was sent to Pontiac reformatory till he is 21 years old. Graham lives in Morgan Park. He broke in the store of Mrs. Turner, entering from the rear, and stole a box of John Ruskin cigars and $5 in small change. He was quickly arrested by the police of the Flintth street station.

COL. DENNISON UP A TREE
The statement published by one of our contemporaries that the Eighth Illinois regiment has a white elephant on its head held in the guardhouse for refusing to enlist at Springfield is literally true. A peculiar feature of the case is that after reaching San Antonio these men concluded to enlist, but upon physical examination were found to be unfit. The only man having the power to release him is the juice wrocade, and it may take months to effect this result through him.

regions.label: text
regions.text: [OCR timeout]

regions.label: text
regions.text: The question now is, how to get the men back home. Who pays the fare? They are not government men. Who pays the mess? They are not in the federal service. Who pays their salaries? The government didn't hire them. That's where the colonel is up a tree. Can you beat it?

regions.label: text

THARPE COMMITS SUICIDE
McKenzie, Tenn., July 21 - Carl Tharpe,
son of Clint Tharpe, committed suicide
Wednesday morning. He had been sick
only a few days. It was thought his ill-
ness played upon his mind. He was left
alone for a few minutes and he quickly
selzed the opportunity to kill himself.

DROPS DEAD MAKING SPEECH
Denison, Texas, July 21 - Alex Nilton
dropped dead while making a talk
to his lodge. Death was due to heart
failure.

goes on to say:
"As he has not seen the 10th cavalry for 34 years, having spent most of that time in Mexico, there is not an enlisted man in the army who knows 'Leut. Flipper can always identify him. He came out of Mexico in 1912, reaching the burying line on March 23rd of that year and has not been in that country since; is loyal to the United States through and through and is ready to fight Mexico or any other foreign country at any time his services are needed by the United States."
He further states that he can produce a thousand Texans to testify that he was not with Gen. Gomez at Carrizal.
Mr. Flipper is to be complimented for his expressions of patriotism and his sentiments along this line are in keeping with the record he made while in the governmental service. We are glad that the report that he was in Gomez's army is intruo.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Railroad Rumblings

regions.label: text
regions.text: G. L. Todd, 5761 Lafayette avenue, is employed by the Pennsylvania Railway Co. as head chef on the Manhattan. Clifton Green, 3405 State street, is employed by the Pennsylvania Railway as second chef between Chicago and Pittsburg, Pa. J. H. Sweetwins, 3575 Wabash avenue, is employed by the Pennsylvania Railway Co. on the Manhattan in charge of a buffet car between Chicago and New York city. John Chambers, 248 East Thirty-seventh street is in the service of the Chicago & Alton Railroad Company as a train porter between Chicago and Kansas City, Mo. L. H. Abel, S. H. Gladden and J. A. McDonald are employed by the Erie Railroad Company at New York city in dining car service between New York and Chicago, Ill. Louis Stith and Roy Rickman are over on the Monon Lines in parlor car service for the Pullman company. "Bulling the Jack." Belved Baker is in the service of the Pero Marquette Railway Co. in parlor car service between Grand Rapids, Mich, and Chicago. James Johnson is holding the position as roast beef student in service of the B. & O. Railway Co. between Chicago and Newcastle Junction, Pa.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Robert Delaney, Ed Bell, Charles Blake, A. H. Miller, Walton John O'Neill, and Sam H. Harris are in the service of the Illinois Central as train and parlor car porters; also James R. Mauplins and Charles Cobb.

3. Box Rhodes, Orick, Mo., R. F. D. No.
4. Box Rhodes, imploded by the Santa Fo Railway Co. as train station manager, James 8 and 15, between Kensas City, Mo., Chicago, Ill. Mr. Rhodes was formerly of Chicago. He is raising chickens and hogs down in the little Missouri town.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
George Washington King, 9 West Fifth street, is employed by the Pullman company over the Chicago & Northwestern Railroad Company's lines to Los Angeles, Cal.

"Old Fifth" is back on the job again. Boys, live and let live; give me your support, and I assure you that you will get the news each week. And I don't want that who stuck "John" Lay it down. It costs money to run a newspaper, and we need the money and cannot live on promises. Phone Oakland 1489, care of John R. Winston.

SICK LIST

Oren Link, 3836 Rhodes avenue, usher at the Dearborn street station, has been ill, but is fast improving.

Andrew Pryor, 4922 State street, is slick at Kankakee, Ill.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Buckhana Lewis, 5400 South Park avenue, is fast improving after an operation at Provident Hospital. Miss Ellisca Hall, 3330 Vernon avenue, is still unable to leave her bed. She was thrown from a street car last April.

J. H. Howard, who has been confined to his home for two months, is slightly improved. Danish Macon, Vernon avenue, is at home from the hospital greatly improved and able to be out. We have friend of 633 Wabash avenue, in front of West Elm hospital. He is looking and is back at work at Armour Institute.
